enrichment.short_description: Cloud business phone system unifying VoIP calling, team messaging, and video meetings for companies of any size.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RingCentral is a cloud communications platform that replaces traditional office phone hardware with a software-based system for calling, SMS, team messaging, and video meetings. Founded in 1999 and headquartered in Belmont, California, the company built its business around unified communications as a service, letting organizations run their entire phone system from a browser, desktop app, or mobile device instead of a PBX closet.

The core product, marketed as RingEX, bundles a business VoIP phone system with auto attendants, call queues, voicemail transcription, and internal team chat in a single subscription. RingCentral also sells adjacent products for video meetings and contact center operations, so a company can start with just phone service and expand into a fuller communications stack as needs grow.

Because it targets everything from five-person offices to large distributed enterprises, RingCentral's plans and add-ons are heavily tiered. That flexibility is a selling point for companies that want one vendor for calling, messaging, and meetings, but it also means the right plan and the total monthly cost depend a lot on headcount, call volume, and which integrations or AI features a team actually needs. As one of the longer-established names in cloud business communications, RingCentral has also had time to build out partnerships with hardware makers, CRM vendors, and telecom carriers across multiple countries, which shows up as broader compliance certifications and international number availability than many newer VoIP entrants offer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who RingCentral Is For
paragraphs: RingCentral fits small and mid-sized businesses that want to retire a legacy desk-phone system without hiring telecom staff to manage it. A single admin console lets a small office manager set up extensions, auto attendants, and call routing rules without needing a background in PBX configuration.

It also serves multi-location businesses and franchises that need consistent phone numbers, call routing, and messaging across branches, since the whole system is centrally administered even when employees are spread across cities or time zones. Sales and support teams that already rely on Salesforce, HubSpot, or Zendesk are a strong fit too, given RingCentral's built-in CRM integrations and call-logging features on its higher tiers.

Remote and hybrid teams that want calling, chat, and video meetings from one login rather than juggling separate tools for each often choose RingCentral for that consolidation. IT teams evaluating a vendor with a long operating history and enterprise-grade compliance and security options also tend to shortlist it alongside other established unified-communications providers. Growing companies that expect to add locations, departments, or headcount over the next few years also benefit from RingCentral's tiered structure, since new users and features can be added without migrating to an entirely different system as the business scales.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Solo founders or very small teams with minimal calling needs may find RingCentral more infrastructure than they require, since per-user monthly pricing and add-on fees for features like AI receptionist or advanced call recording can add up quickly compared to a lightweight VoIP app.

Businesses that need heavy SMS marketing volume out of the box should look closely at the plan limits first, since entry-level tiers cap monthly text messages per user and require an add-on or upgrade to remove that ceiling. Teams whose core need is a full-featured contact center with deep workforce management may also outgrow RingEX and need RingCentral's separate contact center product or a dedicated contact-center vendor instead.

Companies that prioritize a single, simple flat price over configurability may find the multi-tier structure and optional add-ons harder to budget for than a flat-rate competitor. Organizations without any IT support may also want a vendor with more hands-on, done-for-you onboarding rather than a self-serve admin portal.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: RingCentral's foundation is cloud-based VoIP calling with unlimited domestic calling on most plans, along with auto attendants, call queues, call forwarding, and voicemail-to-text transcription. Team messaging is built in, so employees can chat, share files, and manage tasks in the same app used for calls.

Video meetings are included across plans, supporting screen sharing and a meeting-participant capacity that scales with the plan tier. Higher tiers add automatic call recording, call monitoring features like whisper and barge for coaching, and native integrations with CRM and helpdesk tools such as Salesforce, HubSpot, and Zendesk.

RingCentral has also layered AI features into the platform, including AI-generated call and meeting summaries, conversational intelligence for analyzing customer calls, and an AI receptionist add-on that can answer and route calls automatically. An open developer platform and APIs let technical teams build custom integrations or embed RingCentral's calling and messaging capabilities into their own applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: The RingCentral Experience
paragraphs: Day to day, most users interact with RingCentral through its desktop and mobile apps, which combine a phone dialer, message threads, and a meetings launcher in one interface. Admins manage numbers, extensions, and permissions from a separate web-based admin console that does not require any on-site hardware beyond desk phones or headsets employees already have.

Setup typically involves porting existing phone numbers, configuring auto attendants and call queues, and assigning users to a plan tier based on which features they need, such as call recording or CRM integration. Because features and limits vary by tier, teams often spend some time during onboarding mapping departments to the right plan rather than giving everyone the top tier by default.

Ongoing use tends to center on call handling and internal chat, with meetings and AI summaries used more selectively depending on the team. Support is available through in-app resources, a help center, and account-tier-based service levels, and the free trial gives new customers a way to test core calling, messaging, and meeting features before committing to an annual contract.

features.title: Cloud VoIP calling
features.description: Business phone service with unlimited domestic calling, auto attendants, call queues, and call forwarding, all managed from a web admin console.

features.title: Team messaging
features.description: Built-in chat with channels, direct messages, file sharing, and task tracking alongside calling and meetings in the same app.

features.title: Video meetings
features.description: Integrated video conferencing with screen sharing and participant capacity that scales by plan tier.

features.title: Voicemail-to-text
features.description: Automatic transcription of voicemails so users can read messages instead of listening to every recording.

features.title: Call recording and monitoring
features.description: Automatic call recording plus supervisor tools like call whisper and barge on higher-tier plans, useful for coaching sales and support teams.

features.title: CRM and helpdesk integrations
features.description: Native connections to tools such as Salesforce, HubSpot, and Zendesk for logging calls and syncing customer data.

features.title: AI-powered summaries
features.description: AI-generated recaps of calls and meetings intended to reduce manual note-taking for sales and support conversations.

features.title: AI receptionist add-on
features.description: An optional automated attendant that can answer, screen, and route incoming calls without a live employee.

features.title: Developer APIs
features.description: A documented API and SDK set for embedding RingCentral calling, SMS, and messaging into custom internal tools or products.

features.title: Multi-location administration
features.description: Centralized management of numbers, extensions, and routing rules across offices, branches, or remote teams from one console.

pros: Combines phone, messaging, and video meetings in one subscription, reducing the need for separate tools
Scales from small offices to large, multi-location organizations on the same platform
Long operating history in business communications with a broad partner and integration ecosystem
Tiered plans let teams pay for advanced features like call recording only where they are needed
Developer APIs support custom integrations for technical teams
cons: Entry-level plan limits SMS volume and lacks automatic call recording, pushing some teams to upgrade
Add-ons for AI receptionist, conversational intelligence, and other features carry separate costs
Per-user monthly pricing across multiple tiers can make total cost harder to predict than flat-rate alternatives
Advanced contact-center capabilities require a separate product rather than being included in RingEX
Admin console has enough configuration options that setup can take longer than a simpler, lightweight VoIP app

faqs.question: Does RingCentral offer a free plan?
faqs.answer: RingCentral does not offer an ongoing free plan; it offers a limited-time free trial that gives access to core calling, messaging, and meeting features before requiring a paid subscription.

faqs.question: What is the starting price for RingCentral?
faqs.answer: Pricing starts at the Core plan, billed per user per month, with lower rates available when billing annually rather than monthly. Exact pricing can vary by region, number of users, and promotions, so confirming current rates on RingCentral's pricing page is recommended.

faqs.question: Can I keep my existing business phone number with RingCentral?
faqs.answer: Yes, RingCentral supports porting existing phone numbers from other carriers as part of account setup, which is a common step for businesses switching from a legacy phone system.

faqs.question: Is RingCentral suitable for a small business with just a few employees?
faqs.answer: It can work for small teams that want unified calling, messaging, and meetings, though very small teams with light calling needs may find the per-user pricing and add-on fees more than a simpler VoIP tool would cost.

faqs.question: Does RingCentral integrate with CRM tools like Salesforce or HubSpot?
faqs.answer: Yes, RingCentral offers native integrations with popular CRM and helpdesk platforms including Salesforce, HubSpot, and Zendesk, primarily available on its higher-tier plans.

use_cases.title: Replacing a legacy office phone system
use_cases.description: A company retires an on-premises PBX and desk-phone infrastructure in favor of a cloud-based system managed entirely from a web console.
use_cases.audience: Small and mid-sized businesses modernizing office communications

use_cases.title: Unifying multi-location call routing
use_cases.description: A multi-branch business or franchise standardizes phone numbers, auto attendants, and call routing across all locations from a single admin account.

use_cases.title: Supporting a remote or hybrid sales team
use_cases.description: A distributed sales team uses one app for calling leads, messaging teammates, and joining video meetings, with call recording and CRM logging for coaching.
use_cases.audience: Remote and hybrid sales and support teams

use_cases.title: Embedding communications into internal tools
use_cases.description: A technical team uses RingCentral's developer APIs to add click-to-call, SMS, or messaging capabilities directly into an internal application or workflow.
use_cases.audience: Developers and IT teams building custom integrations

features: Video meetings for up to 100 participants
Limited SMS per user per month
CRM integrations with Salesforce, HubSpot, and Zendesk
Unlimited storage for files and recordings

enrichment.seo_meta_description: RingCentral review: pricing, features, and who this cloud VoIP, messaging, and video meetings platform is really built for.

enrichment.short_description: AI-powered video meetings from RingCentral, with a free 100-participant plan and paid tiers unlocking longer meetings, webinars, and full AI transcription.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RingCentral Video is the video meetings product built by RingCentral, Inc., a cloud communications company founded in 1999 and headquartered in Belmont, California. Rather than existing as a standalone startup tool, RingCentral Video is one pillar of a much larger unified communications business that also spans cloud phone systems, team messaging, and contact center software sold under the RingEX and RingCentral MVP umbrellas, giving the product a level of engineering and support resources smaller independent video vendors rarely have. Because it is developed alongside the company's phone and messaging products, it shares infrastructure, security practices, and administrative tooling with the rest of the suite, which can be an advantage for organizations that value a single vendor relationship.

The product's headline draw is a genuinely usable free tier: RingCentral Video Pro lets anyone host meetings with up to 100 participants at no cost, with a 50-minute limit per session. Customers on paid meetings plans or full RingEX subscriptions remove that time cap, gaining unlimited 24-hour meetings for up to 200 participants along with AI-generated transcripts, notes, and recaps built into the price rather than sold as a separate add-on.

Meetings run in a browser with no download required, and dedicated desktop and mobile apps are also available for teams that want a persistent client with notifications and quick-launch shortcuts. Positioned against Zoom, Microsoft Teams, and Google Meet, RingCentral Video's differentiator is less about novel video technology and more about how tightly it folds into a broader phone-and-messaging platform many mid-market and enterprise buyers already run day to day.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who RingCentral Video Is For
paragraphs: Organizations already running RingEX or RingCentral MVP for business phone service are the clearest fit, since video is bundled into those subscriptions rather than billed as a separate line item. For IT teams trying to consolidate vendors, using the video tool that ships with the phone system reduces the number of logins, invoices, and admin consoles staff have to manage across the organization.

Small teams and freelancers who just need a dependable, no-frills meeting tool can also get real value from the free Video Pro tier, since 100 participants and 50 minutes covers the vast majority of internal check-ins, client calls, and interviews without ever entering a credit card or committing to a contract.

Mid-market and enterprise communications teams running larger internal broadcasts, town halls, or customer-facing webinars are another strong match, particularly once they need the AI-generated recaps, live translation, and higher participant ceilings that come with paid plans, along with the analytics needed to prove those sessions are effective.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Teams that need a lightweight, single-purpose video app and nothing else may find RingCentral Video's positioning inside a much larger communications suite unnecessarily heavy. Buyers who only want video, with no phone or contact center component, are often better served evaluating a dedicated conferencing product on its own merits rather than adopting a bundled platform.

Highly price-sensitive users who are not already RingCentral customers may not see enough advantage over free-first competitors like Google Meet, since RingCentral Video's most compelling AI capabilities are reserved for paid or RingEX-bundled plans rather than the free Video Pro tier that most casual users would start with.

Companies planning very large-scale public webinars or events beyond the standard participant ceilings should confirm current capacity and any add-on requirements directly with RingCentral sales, since scaling past the built-in limits typically involves additional licensing conversations rather than a simple self-serve upgrade inside the account settings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, RingCentral Video covers the expected video conferencing basics: screen sharing, an interactive whiteboard with annotations, breakout rooms, virtual backgrounds, and dynamic end-to-end encryption for sensitive sessions. Meetings can be joined directly from a browser or through the desktop and mobile apps, with an audio and video preview before entering the room.

On top of those fundamentals, RingCentral layers AI-driven capabilities: automatic transcription, meeting notes, action-item summaries, and live translation across a wide set of languages for paying customers. Smart scheduling features check attendee availability and can reach guests through their preferred channel when setting up invites, cutting down on scheduling back-and-forth.

For larger-scale communication needs, the product supports webinars and town halls alongside standard meetings, plus built-in analytics that surface meeting quality metrics for administrators. Native calendar integrations with Google Calendar and Outlook keep scheduling inside the tools teams already use day to day, rather than forcing a separate booking workflow, and recordings can be organized and revisited later from cloud storage tied to the account.

long_form_blocks.heading: The RingCentral Video Experience
paragraphs: Joining a RingCentral Video meeting is designed to be frictionless: guests can click a link and enter through a supported web browser without installing anything, while regular users on Windows, Mac, iOS, or Android can rely on native apps for a more integrated experience alongside RingCentral's phone and messaging tools in one client.

Once inside a meeting, the interface favors a familiar layout, with recording, screen sharing, and the whiteboard readily accessible, and AI notetaking running quietly in the background for eligible plans so hosts can focus on the conversation instead of manual note-taking or chasing down follow-up items afterward.

After a call ends, participants on paid or RingEX plans receive AI-generated recaps and searchable transcripts, and recordings can be shared out with a link so anyone who missed the session can catch up quickly. For administrators, the surrounding RingCentral admin console ties video usage and quality analytics into the same dashboard used to manage phone lines and team messaging, simplifying day-to-day oversight and reducing the number of separate systems IT has to monitor.

features.title: AI meeting transcripts and recaps
features.description: Automatic transcription, notes, and action-item summaries are generated after eligible meetings so attendees can review decisions without manual note-taking.

features.title: Live translation
features.description: Paid plans include live translation across a wide range of languages, helping distributed and international teams follow conversations in real time.

features.title: Screen sharing and whiteboard
features.description: Presenters can share their screen and collaborate on an interactive whiteboard with annotation tools during any meeting.

features.title: Breakout rooms
features.description: Hosts can split larger meetings into smaller breakout sessions for focused discussion before bringing everyone back together.

features.title: Virtual backgrounds
features.description: Participants can apply virtual or blurred backgrounds to keep meetings professional regardless of their physical surroundings.

features.title: Meeting recording
features.description: Sessions can be recorded and shared afterward via a link, giving teams a reference for anyone who missed the live meeting.

features.title: Calendar integrations
features.description: Native integrations with Google Calendar and Outlook let users schedule and launch RingCentral Video meetings from the calendar apps they already use.

features.title: Webinars and town halls
features.description: Beyond standard meetings, the platform supports larger broadcast-style webinars and company town halls for one-to-many communication.

features.title: Browser-based join
features.description: Guests can join meetings directly from a supported web browser with no software download required.

features.title: Meeting analytics
features.description: Built-in analytics give administrators visibility into meeting quality and usage trends across the organization.

pros: Free Video Pro tier supports up to 100 participants at no cost
AI-generated transcripts, notes, and recaps reduce manual meeting follow-up
Bundles naturally with RingCentral's broader phone and messaging platform for existing customers
Cross-platform coverage with browser-based join plus Windows, Mac, iOS, and Android apps
Supports webinars and town halls in addition to standard team meetings
cons: Free plan meetings are capped at 50 minutes
The most useful AI features are limited to paid or RingEX-bundled plans
Pricing and packaging can be confusing to evaluate outside the context of a full RingEX subscription
Less compelling as a pure standalone video tool compared with dedicated conferencing specialists
Scaling beyond standard participant limits for large events typically requires a direct sales conversation

faqs.question: Is RingCentral Video free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Video Pro tier is free and supports meetings with up to 100 participants, though sessions are limited to 50 minutes each.

faqs.question: How is RingCentral Video different from RingEX?
faqs.answer: RingEX is RingCentral's broader unified communications platform, covering phone, messaging, and video. RingCentral Video is the video meetings component, which is included when you subscribe to RingEX or purchased on its own paid tier.

faqs.question: Does RingCentral Video offer live translation?
faqs.answer: Yes, paid plans include live translation across a wide range of languages, which is aimed at distributed and international teams.

faqs.question: Do meeting guests need to create an account or download software to join?
faqs.answer: No. Guests can join a RingCentral Video meeting directly from a supported web browser without installing anything, though desktop and mobile apps are also available.

faqs.question: What is the maximum number of meeting participants?
faqs.answer: The free Video Pro plan supports up to 100 participants, while paid and RingEX-bundled plans raise that ceiling to up to 200 participants.

use_cases.title: Daily team standups
use_cases.description: Distributed teams use the free or paid tier for recurring check-ins, relying on screen sharing and recording for anyone who cannot attend live.

use_cases.title: Client and sales calls
use_cases.description: Sales and consulting teams host client-facing video calls and lean on AI transcripts and recaps to capture commitments and follow-up items.

use_cases.title: Company town halls and webinars
use_cases.description: Internal communications teams broadcast all-hands meetings and external webinars using the platform's larger-capacity meeting and analytics tools.

use_cases.title: Consolidating unified communications
use_cases.description: IT departments already running RingEX for phone service extend usage to RingCentral Video to reduce the number of separate communication vendors.

features: Full AI transcripts, notes, and recaps
RingCentral Video included alongside cloud phone and messaging

enrichment.seo_meta_description: RingCentral Video review: free plan limits, AI transcription, pricing tiers, pros and cons, and who this video meetings tool is really built for.

enrichment.short_description: All-in-one workforce platform unifying HR, payroll, IT device management, and spend management around one employee record.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom pricing based on modules and employee count (contact sales for a quote)

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Rippling is a workforce management platform that combines human resources, payroll, IT device management, benefits administration, and business spend management into a single system built around one unified employee record. Founded in 2016 by Parker Conrad and Prasanna Sankar, the company set out to solve a problem common to growing businesses: employee data scattered across dozens of disconnected tools, each requiring its own manual updates whenever someone joins, changes roles, or leaves.

The core idea behind Rippling is that most workforce processes, provisioning a laptop, enrolling in benefits, updating a paycheck, granting software access, are really the same underlying event: a change to an employee's record. By centralizing that record and layering modular products on top of it, Rippling lets a single action, like hiring someone or changing their department, automatically cascade through payroll, benefits, IT systems, and finance without duplicate data entry.

This positions Rippling as a direct alternative to running separate best-of-breed tools for HRIS, payroll, identity and device management, and expense management. It competes with a mix of specialists in each category, from payroll-first providers to IT-focused mobile device management tools, by arguing that unification, rather than depth in any single module, is what growing, increasingly distributed companies need most.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Rippling Is For
paragraphs: Rippling tends to fit growing mid-market companies, roughly fifty to a few thousand employees, that have outgrown a basic payroll provider or spreadsheet-driven HR process and are actively adding new tools, locations, or entities. Businesses expanding into new states or countries, and needing to keep compliance, payroll, and benefits synchronized across jurisdictions, are a natural match for the platform's unified data model.

IT-heavy organizations are another strong fit. Companies that issue and manage a fleet of laptops, provision dozens of SaaS applications per new hire, and need to instantly revoke access when someone departs benefit from Rippling's device and identity management tying directly into HR events, rather than IT running a separate offboarding checklist by hand.

Finance and operations leaders who want visibility into how headcount changes affect spend, corporate cards, expense reports, and vendor bills alongside payroll, also gravitate toward Rippling. For teams that already juggle HR, IT, and finance stacks that don't talk to each other, consolidating onto one employee-record-driven platform is often the primary appeal.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Very small businesses and solo founders with only a handful of employees may find Rippling more platform than they need. The modular, per-employee pricing structure and onboarding process are built with scaling organizations in mind, and a lighter standalone payroll or HR tool can often cover a tiny team's needs at a lower total cost and with less setup.

Companies that prioritize transparent, self-serve pricing may also be frustrated by Rippling's sales-led, custom-quote model. Because costs depend on which modules are activated and how many employees are enrolled in each, buyers who want to see an exact price online before talking to anyone will need to go through a sales conversation to get real numbers.

Organizations that are happy with their existing best-of-breed tools for payroll, IT, or expense management, and don't want to migrate historical data or retrain staff on a new system, may not see enough incremental value in consolidation to justify a switch. Rippling's advantage is most pronounced when a company is actively suffering from tool sprawl, not when its current stack is already working well.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, Rippling offers HR and payroll functionality: employee records, onboarding and offboarding workflows, time and attendance tracking, and payroll processing that can span multiple states and, through additional modules, multiple countries. Benefits administration is built on top of the same employee data, letting open enrollment and life-event changes flow directly into payroll deductions.

On the IT side, Rippling can manage laptops and other devices, push software and security policies, and provision or deprovision access to third-party applications the moment someone's employment status changes. This device and identity management layer is one of the platform's more differentiated offerings compared with HR-only competitors.

Rippling also extends into finance with spend management tools covering corporate cards, expense reimbursement, and bill pay, and it offers a no-code workflow builder that lets administrators automate custom approval chains and notifications across HR, IT, and finance events. Global employer-of-record and international payroll options round out the platform for companies with employees outside their home country.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Rippling Experience
paragraphs: New customers typically go through a guided implementation with a Rippling representative to migrate historical payroll and employee data, configure the modules they've purchased, and connect any existing third-party applications. The initial setup can take meaningful time for companies importing years of payroll history or complex org structures, but the payoff is a system where most day-to-day HR and IT actions become a single form submission rather than several.

Once configured, the day-to-day interface is generally described as clean and modern relative to older HR software, with a dashboard that surfaces pending approvals, upcoming payroll runs, and compliance tasks in one place. The workflow automation builder is a standout for administrators who want to codify approval logic without writing code, though building anything sophisticated does involve a learning curve.

Ongoing support is typically delivered through account representatives for larger customers and a help center plus support tickets for smaller ones. Overall sentiment from users tends to be positive about the breadth of what's unified under one roof, with the most common friction points being the sales-driven pricing process and the initial data migration effort rather than the day-to-day product experience itself.

features.title: Unified employee record
features.description: A single source of truth for employee data that automatically syncs changes across payroll, benefits, IT, and finance modules.

features.title: Payroll
features.description: Automated payroll processing with support for multi-state operations and additional modules for international payroll.

features.title: Benefits administration
features.description: Open enrollment, life-event changes, and benefits deductions tied directly into the payroll engine.

features.title: Device management
features.description: Remote configuration, software deployment, and security policy enforcement across company-owned laptops and other devices.

features.title: App and identity management
features.description: Automatic provisioning and deprovisioning of third-party software access based on employee status and role changes.

features.title: Spend management
features.description: Corporate cards, expense reimbursement, and bill pay tools connected to the same employee and department data.

features.title: No-code workflow builder
features.description: A visual automation tool for building custom approval chains, notifications, and cross-module triggers without writing code.

features.title: Time and attendance
features.description: Time tracking, scheduling, and PTO management that feeds directly into payroll calculations.

features.title: Global employer of record (EOR)
features.description: Options for hiring and paying international employees or contractors without setting up a local legal entity.

features.title: Reporting and analytics
features.description: Custom reports across headcount, payroll, spend, and IT usage built from the unified employee dataset.

pros: Consolidates HR, IT, payroll, and finance data into one employee record, reducing duplicate data entry
Device and app provisioning/deprovisioning tied directly to HR events speeds up onboarding and offboarding
No-code workflow automation lets admins build custom cross-department processes without engineering help
Modular structure allows companies to add capabilities like spend management or global payroll as they grow
Modern, relatively clean interface compared with many legacy HR and payroll systems
cons: Pricing is not published and requires a custom sales quote, making upfront budgeting harder
Initial setup and data migration can be time-consuming for companies with complex historical payroll data
Per-module, per-employee costs can add up quickly for organizations that need most of the platform's capabilities
May be more platform than very small businesses or solo founders actually need
Building advanced custom workflows has a learning curve for non-technical admins

faqs.question: How much does Rippling cost?
faqs.answer: Rippling does not publish fixed public pricing. Costs are based on which modules (HR, payroll, benefits, IT, spend management, global payroll, etc.) a company activates and how many employees are enrolled, so businesses need to contact Rippling's sales team for a custom quote.

faqs.question: Is Rippling only for large enterprises?
faqs.answer: No, Rippling is used by companies of varying sizes, but it is generally best suited to growing mid-market businesses with dozens to a few thousand employees rather than very small teams, given its modular, per-employee pricing structure.

faqs.question: Can Rippling handle payroll in multiple states or countries?
faqs.answer: Yes, Rippling supports multi-state payroll in the US, and offers additional modules for international payroll and employer-of-record services for hiring employees in other countries.

faqs.question: Does Rippling replace our existing IT device management tools?
faqs.answer: For many customers, yes. Rippling's IT module can manage laptops and other devices, enforce security policies, and provision or revoke software access automatically based on employment status, which can replace standalone mobile device management tools.

faqs.question: How long does it take to implement Rippling?
faqs.answer: Implementation time varies by company size and how many modules are being adopted at once; most customers work with a Rippling representative to migrate historical data and configure the platform, which can take anywhere from a few weeks to longer for complex, multi-entity organizations.

use_cases.title: Consolidating HR, IT, and payroll onto one platform
use_cases.description: A growing company replaces separate HRIS, payroll, and device management tools with Rippling so that a single change to an employee record updates pay, benefits, and system access everywhere at once.

use_cases.title: Automating onboarding and offboarding
use_cases.description: New hires get their laptop configuration, software access, and benefits enrollment triggered automatically the moment they're added to the system, and access is revoked instantly on their last day.

use_cases.title: Managing a multi-state or global workforce
use_cases.description: A company with employees across several US states, or expanding internationally, uses Rippling's payroll and employer-of-record options to stay compliant without standing up new local entities.

use_cases.title: Connecting spend management to payroll
use_cases.description: Finance teams issue corporate cards and manage expense reports through Rippling, tying spend data to the same department and employee records used for payroll and budgeting.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Rippling review: an all-in-one HR, IT, payroll, and spend management platform. See features, pricing, pros, cons, and who it's best for.

enrichment.short_description: Cloud-based legal practice management software for law firms, covering billing, time tracking, trust accounting, and matter management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Rocket Matter is a cloud-based legal practice management platform built for law firms that need to track time, bill clients, manage trust accounts, and keep matters organized in one system. Founded in 2007 and headquartered in Boca Raton, Florida, the company has spent more than a decade focused specifically on the day-to-day workflows of legal professionals rather than on general-purpose project management or accounting software.

Since 2020, Rocket Matter has operated as part of ProfitSolv, a group of legal and accounting software companies that also includes TimeSolv and ImagineTime. That ownership change has not altered the product's core focus: helping firms capture billable time, invoice clients accurately, and manage the financial side of running a law practice without stitching together spreadsheets, separate billing tools, and email.

The platform runs entirely in a web browser, with companion iOS and Android apps so attorneys can log time, review matters, or check calendars away from their desks. Pricing is structured as a per-user monthly subscription split across four tiers, with a free trial available so firms can test the software's matter management, billing, and trust accounting tools before committing to an annual contract. Rocket Matter also lists SOC 2 Type 2 and PCI certifications, along with end-to-end encryption, which matter to firms that handle sensitive client records and client trust funds.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Rocket Matter Is For
paragraphs: Rocket Matter is aimed primarily at solo attorneys and small-to-midsize law firms that want a single system for running daily operations, from opening a new matter through sending the final invoice. Firms in practice areas such as personal injury, family law, estate planning, real estate, and general business law are common users, since the software's matter templates and workflows can be configured around those specific practice types.

It also suits firms that are outgrowing generic tools like spreadsheets, shared email inboxes, and standalone billing software, and want trust accounting, time tracking, and case management combined into one connected system. Because pricing scales by tier, a growing firm can start on the lower-cost Essentials plan and move up to add features like advanced billing permissions, matter budgets, or full legal accounting as headcount and case volume increase.

Firms that already keep their books in QuickBooks tend to find Rocket Matter appealing because of its accounting integration, which cuts down on duplicate data entry between legal billing and the general ledger. Mid-sized firms with several timekeepers who need shared calendars, task assignment, and centralized reporting across many open matters are also a strong fit for the platform, particularly once they outgrow the lowest pricing tier and need matter-based trust accounts or custom rate management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Very large law firms, or firms with highly specialized enterprise requirements such as conflict-checking across hundreds of attorneys or deep integration with an existing enterprise resource planning system, may find Rocket Matter's feature set oriented more toward small and mid-sized practices than toward large-firm operations with dedicated IT departments.

Firms outside the legal industry, such as accounting or general professional services businesses, are generally better served by one of ProfitSolv's other products or by a general-purpose practice management tool, since Rocket Matter's workflows, terminology, and compliance features are built specifically around legal trust accounting and matter-based billing rules like IOLTA compliance.

Solo practitioners or very small firms on a tight budget who only need basic time tracking or simple invoicing, without trust accounting or full matter management, may find the per-user monthly pricing harder to justify compared with lighter, lower-cost billing tools that skip the broader practice management feature set entirely.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities include matter and case management, time and expense tracking, legal billing, and trust accounting, all built to support the IOLTA compliance requirements that govern how law firms handle client funds. Firms can track billable and non-billable time from a desktop browser or a mobile app, then turn that time into an invoice without re-entering data.

Higher tiers add flat-fee billing, matter-based trust accounts, custom rate management, matter budgets, split billing, fee allocation, and advanced user or billing permissions, giving larger firms more control over how revenue and trust funds are tracked across partners, associates, and practice groups.

Beyond billing, Rocket Matter includes calendaring, task management, contact management, customizable dashboards, saved views, electronic signatures, document sharing, a built-in legal CRM for business development, and reporting tools, along with a QuickBooks integration for firms that keep their general ledger outside the platform. Optional add-ons, such as a more advanced legal CRM tier and website hosting, let firms extend the base subscription without switching to a different product.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Rocket Matter Experience
paragraphs: Day to day, Rocket Matter is designed to feel like a single dashboard a lawyer can return to throughout the day rather than a set of disconnected modules. Matters, contacts, calendar entries, tasks, and time entries are all linked, so opening a case shows the relevant documents, deadlines, and billing history in one view instead of requiring a search across separate tools.

The interface leans on customizable dashboards and saved views so different roles at a firm, such as an associate tracking their own time versus a billing administrator reviewing firm-wide invoices, can see the information most relevant to them without extra configuration. Mobile apps extend that same experience so time entry and matter updates are not limited to the office.

Because the product is delivered as a subscription with tiered plans, the overall experience also depends on which tier a firm selects: Essentials covers core matter management and billing, while Pro, Premier, and Elite progressively unlock more automation, permissions, and legal accounting depth, meaning the day-to-day feel of the software can change meaningfully depending on plan level.

features.title: Legal Billing and Invoicing

features.title: Trust Accounting
features.description: Manages client trust funds with matter-based trust accounts and controls designed to support IOLTA compliance requirements.

features.title: Legal CRM
features.description: Tracks leads and referral sources and supports business development workflows, with an add-on tier that includes marketing automation.

features.title: Calendaring and Task Management
features.description: Provides shared calendars and task assignment so deadlines and responsibilities across a matter are visible to the whole team.

features.title: Document Automation and eSignatures
features.description: Generates documents from templates and supports electronic signature collection directly within a matter.

features.title: Reporting and Dashboards
features.description: Offers customizable dashboards and saved views so different roles, from associates to billing administrators, can monitor the metrics relevant to them.

features.title: QuickBooks Integration
features.description: Synchronizes billing and accounting data with QuickBooks to reduce duplicate entry between legal billing and the general ledger.

features.title: Mobile Apps
features.description: Native iOS and Android apps let attorneys track time, review matters, and check calendars away from the office.

Tiered pricing lets firms start small and add advanced billing or accounting features as they grow
Native iOS and Android apps support time tracking and matter review away from the office
QuickBooks integration reduces duplicate data entry between billing and bookkeeping
Backing from ProfitSolv provides continuity alongside sibling products like TimeSolv
cons: Advanced features such as matter budgets, split billing, and full legal accounting are reserved for higher-priced tiers

Feature set is tailored to legal workflows, offering limited value outside law firm use cases
Some capabilities, like the enterprise CRM add-on, carry additional cost beyond the base subscription
Firms with highly specialized enterprise or multi-office requirements may need customization beyond what the platform offers out of the box

faqs.question: What is Rocket Matter used for?
faqs.answer: Rocket Matter is legal practice management software that law firms use to manage matters, track time, bill clients, and handle trust accounting in one system.

faqs.question: Does Rocket Matter offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Rocket Matter offers a free trial so firms can evaluate matter management, billing, and trust accounting features before subscribing to a paid plan.

faqs.question: How is Rocket Matter priced?
faqs.answer: Rocket Matter uses per-user monthly pricing across four tiers, Essentials, Pro, Premier, and Elite, with lower rates available on annual billing.

faqs.question: Is Rocket Matter available on mobile devices?
faqs.answer: Yes, Rocket Matter provides native iOS and Android apps so attorneys can track time and review matters away from their desks.

faqs.question: Who owns Rocket Matter?
faqs.answer: Rocket Matter has been owned by ProfitSolv since 2020, a group that also owns legal and accounting software companies TimeSolv and ImagineTime.

use_cases.title: Small firm handling client trust funds

use_cases.title: Mid-sized firm coordinating multiple timekeepers

use_cases.title: Firm syncing legal billing with bookkeeping

enrichment.short_description: A resource and capacity planning platform for project-based teams to schedule staff, track utilization, and forecast project financials in real time.
enrichment.starting_price: $7 per person/month (Lite plan, billed monthly); free plan available for teams of 5 or fewer
enrichment.company_size: Small (approximately 50-100 employees, distributed across multiple continents)

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Runn is a resource planning and forecasting platform built for project-based businesses that need to schedule people, track capacity, and understand project financials in one place. Rather than managing day-to-day tasks, Runn focuses on the layer above execution: who is working on what, how much of their time is committed, and what that means for revenue and margin across the whole portfolio of work.

The product centers on a visual, drag-and-drop schedule that shows every person and every project on a single timeline. Managers can see at a glance who is overbooked, who has spare capacity, and how upcoming work will affect utilization weeks or months ahead, which supports both short-term staffing decisions and longer-term hiring and pipeline planning conversations with leadership.

Founded in 2018 and headquartered in Wellington, New Zealand, Runn has grown into a tool used by agencies, consultancies, and technology teams around the world. It is typically adopted alongside existing project management and accounting software, filling the resourcing and forecasting gap those tools do not cover, and connecting scheduling data to the financial reality of running a project-based business. This positioning has helped Runn build a reputation as a focused resourcing layer rather than a broad, do-everything suite.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Runn Is For
paragraphs: Runn is best suited to project-based organizations that staff multiple concurrent projects with a shared pool of people, such as digital agencies, consulting firms, engineering studios, and other professional services businesses. These teams need to know, in real time, who is available, who is stretched thin, and how staffing choices affect delivery timelines and profitability across every active engagement. Multi-disciplinary teams that move people between client accounts on a weekly or monthly basis are a particularly strong fit.

It also appeals to operations and resourcing managers who are responsible for allocating people across projects and want a single source of truth for capacity, rather than piecing that view together from spreadsheets or task boards. Finance leaders and executives benefit too, since Runn ties scheduling directly to project budgets and revenue forecasts, turning a staffing decision into a visible financial one.

Teams that bill clients by the hour or track utilization as a core business metric tend to get the most value, because Runn was built around exactly that workflow: matching people to billable work, watching utilization trends over time, and forecasting how the pipeline of tentative and confirmed projects will affect staffing needs and revenue in the coming quarters.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Runn is not a task-management or project-execution tool, so teams looking for kanban boards, task lists, or sprint tracking will still need separate software for that layer of work. Organizations hoping for a single all-in-one platform that replaces both task management and resourcing may find Runn only covers part of that need and requires pairing with another tool.

Very small teams or solo freelancers may find the paid tiers less economical, since paid plans are priced per resource seat and typically carry a minimum seat requirement; the free plan covers only teams of 5 or fewer managed people, which limits how far an organization can grow before paid pricing applies and before the per-seat cost becomes a meaningful line item. Teams that only occasionally need to plan resourcing may find the ongoing per-seat cost hard to justify compared with an ad hoc spreadsheet.

Companies that need heavier customization, advanced security controls such as SSO, or deep custom reporting out of the box should note that these capabilities sit on the higher-priced Advanced tier, which requires contacting sales rather than self-serve signup, so budget-constrained teams evaluating those specific features should plan for that sales conversation in advance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At the core of Runn is its scheduling grid, where every person, project, and assignment lives on a shared timeline. Capacity and utilization charts sit alongside the schedule so managers can immediately see the effect of adding or moving an assignment, including whether it pushes someone into overbooked territory or leaves a specialist underused for the week.

Project financials are built into the same view: budgets, costs, and revenue are tracked per project, and forecasting tools project how confirmed and tentative work will affect future utilization and revenue. Scenario planning lets teams model draft or unconfirmed projects against real capacity before committing resources, so a sales pipeline can be tested against the team's actual availability.

Beyond scheduling and forecasting, Runn supports timesheets for comparing planned versus actual hours, skills and tags for matching people to the right work, and integrations with tools such as Harvest, ClickUp, Asana, and accounting platforms through its API. Higher tiers add unlimited custom fields, custom insight dashboards, an audit activity log, and SAML single sign-on for larger organizations. This combination lets larger organizations enforce their own data structures and reporting standards on top of Runn's core scheduling engine.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Runn Experience
paragraphs: Day to day, using Runn feels closer to working with a live staffing spreadsheet than a traditional project management app. The interface is visual and color-coded, with people and projects arranged as rows so patterns of over- and under-utilization are easy to spot without digging through separate reports or exporting data elsewhere.

New users generally find the core scheduling workflow quick to pick up, since most actions are a matter of dragging an assignment onto the timeline and adjusting its length or hours. The financial and forecasting views build on that same underlying data, so once a schedule is populated, budget and revenue projections update automatically without extra manual data entry. Support resources and a searchable help center are also available for teams getting started with more advanced configuration.

Because Runn is delivered entirely as a web application, it works the same way across devices with a modern browser, though there is no dedicated native mobile app for iOS or Android. Teams typically run it in parallel with their existing project management, accounting, and HR tools, feeding data in and out through integrations or the open API rather than replacing that stack outright.

features.title: Forecasting

features.title: Scenario Planning

features.title: Integrations and API

features.title: Custom Fields and Dashboards

features.title: Audit Log and SSO
features.description: Standard and Advanced plans add an activity audit log and SAML single sign-on for larger, security-conscious teams.

pros: Free plan available for small teams with no credit card required
Intuitive drag-and-drop scheduling that is easy to learn
Strong project financial forecasting built directly into the schedule
Scenario planning lets teams test tentative staffing before committing
Integrates with existing project management and accounting tools rather than replacing them
cons: Not a task-management tool, so teams still need separate software for day-to-day task tracking
Paid plans require a minimum number of resource seats, which can be costly for very small paid teams
Advanced plan pricing is not published and requires contacting sales
Timesheets, integrations, and API access are gated behind the Standard tier and above
No native mobile apps; the product is web-based only

faqs.question: Does Runn have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Runn offers a free plan for teams managing 5 or fewer people, with no credit card required to start.

faqs.question: What is the difference between the Lite and Standard plans?
faqs.answer: Standard includes everything in Lite plus timesheets, custom fields, integrations, API access, and an audit log.

faqs.question: Does Runn integrate with other tools?
faqs.answer: Yes, Runn connects with tools such as Harvest, ClickUp, Asana, and accounting systems, and offers an open API on Standard and Advanced plans.

faqs.question: Is there a minimum team size to use Runn?
faqs.answer: The free plan supports up to 5 people; paid plans generally require a minimum number of resource seats, so very small paid teams should check current terms on the pricing page.

faqs.question: Does Runn replace project management software like Asana or Jira?
faqs.answer: No, Runn focuses on resource scheduling, capacity planning, and financial forecasting, and is designed to work alongside task-management tools rather than replace them.

enrichment.short_description: Runway is an AI platform for generating and editing video, image, and audio content, including text-to-video, motion capture, and voice tools.
enrichment.headquarters: New York City, New York, USA

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Runway, also known as RunwayML, is a New York based AI research and product company, founded in 2018, that builds generative tools for creating and editing video, image, and audio content. Its core product is a browser based suite that pairs proprietary generative video models, currently branded Gen-4.5, with an editor for turning text prompts, still images, or existing footage into new video clips, giving individual creators access to production techniques that once required much larger crews and budgets.

The company was started by Cristobal Valenzuela, Anastasis Germanidis, and Alejandro Matamala after meeting at New York University's Tisch School of the Arts ITP program, and it has since grown into one of the more recognized names in AI video generation, competing alongside tools from much larger labs while remaining an independently operated, venture backed company headquartered in New York City with a growing team of researchers, engineers, and product staff.

Beyond text-to-video and image-to-video generation, Runway packages a set of adjacent tools, motion tracking, green screen removal, inpainting, camera control, lip sync, and voice generation, into a single workspace, positioning itself less as a single model and more as an end to end creative production environment for AI generated media, spanning early concept work through final export of a finished clip.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Runway Is For
paragraphs: Runway is aimed at filmmakers, video editors, and motion designers who want to generate b-roll, concept footage, or stylized shots without a full production crew, camera equipment, or location shoot, along with marketing and social teams that need a steady output of short video ads, product clips, or promotional content on a compressed timeline and limited budget.

Independent creators and small studios exploring AI filmmaking use Runway to storyboard, previsualize, or fully produce short experimental pieces, while agencies use it to prototype concepts for clients quickly before committing to traditional production budgets and schedules, letting a small team present several visual directions in the time a single traditional shoot would take.

Product and brand teams that need repeatable, on-brand visual content, along with educators and researchers experimenting with generative media, also find Runway's mix of video, image, and audio tools useful for rapid iteration inside a single workspace rather than stitching together several separate applications, since a project can move from a text prompt to a shareable clip within one browser tab.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Teams that need frame accurate, fully controllable footage, such as broadcast production requiring exact continuity or precise, repeatable actor performances, will find generative video's inherent unpredictability a poor fit, since outputs can drift from a prompt in ways traditional filming or manual animation would not, and reshoots are prompt based rather than fully directable in the way a camera operator can be.

High volume users planning to generate long-form video at scale should evaluate the credit economics carefully, since every generated second consumes credits, and costs can climb quickly for teams producing many long clips a month, making Runway less cost efficient than traditional stock footage or in-house filming for large scale, repetitive production needs.

Organizations with strict compliance or data residency requirements, or those unwilling to work through occasional visual artifacts and inconsistencies common to current generative video models, may prefer to wait for more mature tooling or pair Runway with heavier human review before relying on it for mission critical or heavily regulated content, particularly in advertising, healthcare, legal, or financial contexts where accuracy expectations are especially high.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At the center of Runway is its generative video model line, currently Gen-4.5, which supports text-to-video, image-to-video, and video-to-video generation, alongside Act-Two for transferring an actor's performance onto a generated character and Frames for high fidelity AI image generation with consistent style controls across a project.

The editing toolkit includes green screen and background removal without a physical green screen, a motion brush and camera controls for directing movement within a generated shot, inpainting and object removal for cleaning up footage, and custom voice and lip sync tools for adding narration or dialogue to generated characters, all accessible from the same project window.

Runway also ships a timeline based video editor for assembling generated and uploaded clips, a shared asset library and workspace for team collaboration, and an API for developers who want to call Runway's models programmatically from their own applications rather than the web interface, which supports building custom tools, integrations, and automated content pipelines around the same underlying models used in the consumer product.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Runway Experience
paragraphs: Runway's interface is entirely browser based, organized around a project workspace where users prompt for generations, review results, and drag clips into a lightweight timeline editor, so no local software installation is required, and most actions, from typing a prompt to exporting a clip, happen within a few clicks on a fairly approachable, uncluttered layout.

Every generation draws from a monthly credit allowance that scales with the paid plan, and the free tier offers a limited one-time credit grant, which lets new users test the core generation quality, review turnaround time, and general workflow before deciding whether the output justifies moving to a paid subscription.

The overall experience rewards iteration and experimentation: generations are quick, prompts often need a few attempts to get right, and the company pushes new models and features often, so the platform can feel like a moving target, though it has generally kept the same core workspace and pricing structure since launch, which keeps the learning curve manageable for returning users familiar with earlier model generations.

features.description: Turn a still image into an animated video clip with controllable motion.

features.title: Green Screen
features.description: Remove or replace video backgrounds without needing a physical green screen setup.

features.description: Direct which parts of a generated scene move and how the camera pans, zooms, or tracks.

features.title: Inpainting and Object Removal
features.description: Erase or replace unwanted objects and elements within existing footage.

features.title: Custom Voice and Lip Sync
features.description: Generate speech and synchronize it to a character's mouth movements.

features.title: API Access
features.description: Call Runway's generative models programmatically for custom integrations and workflows.

All-in-one workspace covering video, image, and audio generation plus editing tools
Frequent model updates that improve quality and add new capabilities
Browser based, so no installation or heavy local hardware is required
Free plan lets new users test generation quality before subscribing
cons: Credit costs can add up quickly for teams producing high volumes of long clips
Generated footage can be unpredictable and occasionally include visual artifacts
Limited fine-grained control compared to traditional filming or manual animation
Storage limits and advanced features are gated behind higher priced tiers
Frequent plan and model changes can make long-term cost planning harder

faqs.question: What is Runway used for?
faqs.answer: Runway is used to generate and edit AI video, image, and audio content, including text-to-video and image-to-video clips, for film, marketing, and general content creation.

faqs.question: Does Runway have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Runway offers a free plan with a one-time credit allowance so new users can try its generation tools before subscribing to a paid plan.

faqs.question: How does Runway's credit system work?
faqs.answer: Each generation consumes credits based on output length and the model used, and paid plans include a monthly credit allowance that scales with the plan tier.

faqs.question: Can I use Runway through an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, Runway provides an API that lets developers call its generative video and image models programmatically from their own applications.

faqs.question: Is Runway suitable for professional film production?
faqs.answer: Runway is used by professionals for previsualization, concept work, and stylized shots, but frame-accurate control needed for some broadcast or continuity-heavy work may still require traditional production methods.

use_cases.title: AI Short Film and Concept Production
use_cases.description: Creators use Runway's text-to-video and image-to-video tools to previsualize scenes or produce full experimental short films without a traditional shoot.

use_cases.title: Marketing and Social Video Content
use_cases.description: Marketing teams generate short product, promotional, or social clips quickly to keep pace with campaign and posting schedules.

use_cases.title: Agency Concept Prototyping
use_cases.description: Agencies use Runway to mock up visual concepts and pitch ideas to clients before committing to full production budgets.

use_cases.title: Programmatic Video Generation
use_cases.description: Developers integrate Runway's API into their own apps or pipelines to generate video and image assets at scale.
use_cases.audience: Developers and product teams building custom workflows
